quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "One-to-one function"?

options.text: Priority for choosing u: Logarithmic > Inverse trig > Algebraic > Trigonometric > Exponential; u is differentiated, dv is integrated
d/dx[cf(x)] = c·f'(x); constants factor out of the derivative
F(x) = ∫ₐˣ f(t)dt; represents the accumulated area from a to x; its derivative is f(x) by FTC Part 1
A function where each output value corresponds to exactly one input value; passes the horizontal line test

quiz_questions.explanation: "One-to-one function" is defined as: A function where each output value corresponds to exactly one input value; passes the horizontal line test. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Inverse function"?

options.text: Derived from the limit definition using sin(x+h) = sin x cos h + cos x sin h and special limits
f⁻¹ reverses f: if f(a) = b, then f⁻¹(b) = a; domain of f⁻¹ = range of f, and vice versa
Take ln of both sides, differentiate implicitly, solve for dy/dx; essential when both base and exponent are variable
P(t) = K/(1 + Ae^(-rt)) where A = (K-P₀)/P₀; produces an S-shaped (sigmoidal) curve

quiz_questions.explanation: "Inverse function" is defined as: f⁻¹ reverses f: if f(a) = b, then f⁻¹(b) = a; domain of f⁻¹ = range of f, and vice versa. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Horizontal line test"?

options.text: Left: sample at left endpoint; Right: sample at right endpoint; Midpoint: sample at center of each subinterval
From a graph, check if the function approaches the same value from the left and right; if not, the two-sided limit does not exist
At a critical point c: f' changes + to -: local max; f' changes - to +: local min; no sign change: neither
A function is one-to-one if and only if every horizontal line intersects its graph at most once

quiz_questions.explanation: "Horizontal line test" is defined as: A function is one-to-one if and only if every horizontal line intersects its graph at most once. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Inverse function derivative"?

options.text: EVT guarantees extrema exist but does not tell us where; we use critical point analysis and endpoint evaluation to locate them
The interval must include both endpoints; on open intervals (a,b), extrema may be approached but not attained
(f⁻¹)'(a) = 1/f'(f⁻¹(a)); the derivative of the inverse at a point is the reciprocal of the derivative of f at the corresponding point
From a graph, check if the function approaches the same value from the left and right; if not, the two-sided limit does not exist

quiz_questions.explanation: "Inverse function derivative" is defined as: (f⁻¹)'(a) = 1/f'(f⁻¹(a)); the derivative of the inverse at a point is the reciprocal of the derivative of f at the corresponding point. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Restricting domain"?

options.text: Motion along a straight line (one dimension); described completely by position as a function of time s(t)
d/dx[cf(x)] = c·f'(x); constants factor out of the derivative
Non-one-to-one functions can be made invertible by restricting the domain (e.g., sin x restricted to [-π/2, π/2] gives arcsin)
Find dy/dt given dx/dt (or vice versa) by differentiating an equation relating x and y with respect to time t

quiz_questions.explanation: "Restricting domain" is defined as: Non-one-to-one functions can be made invertible by restricting the domain (e.g., sin x restricted to [-π/2, π/2] gives arcsin). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A function where each output value corresponds to exactly one input value; passes the horizontal line test"?

options.text: Closed interval method
One-to-one function
d/dx[cos x] = -sin x
Infinite limit

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "One-to-one function".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "f⁻¹ reverses f: if f(a) = b, then f⁻¹(b) = a; domain of f⁻¹ = range of f, and vice versa"?

options.text: Inverse function
Equilibrium solutions
Related rates method
Shadow problems

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Inverse function".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A function is one-to-one if and only if every horizontal line intersects its graph at most once"?

options.text: Zero derivative implies constant
Exponential function
Horizontal line test
d/dx[cos x] = -sin x

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Horizontal line test".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "(f⁻¹)'(a) = 1/f'(f⁻¹(a)); the derivative of the inverse at a point is the reciprocal of the derivative of f at the corresponding point"?

options.text: Verifying a solution
Newton's method
Notation
Inverse function derivative

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Inverse function derivative".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Non-one-to-one functions can be made invertible by restricting the domain (e.g., sin x restricted to [-π/2, π/2] gives arcsin)"?

options.text: Cumulative distribution function (CDF)
Verification technique
d/dx[cos x] = -sin x
Restricting domain

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Restricting domain".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about One-to-One Functions and Inverses is accurate?

options.text: The inflection point occurs at P = K/2, where the population is growing fastest.
One-to-One Functions and Inverses are functions where each output corresponds to exactly one input: f(a) = f(b) implies a = b.
When k > 0: exponential growth (population, investment); when k < 0: exponential decay (radioactive decay, drug clearance).
It is the theoretical foundation for many calculus results.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes One-to-One Functions and Inverses as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of One-to-One Functions and Inverses?

options.text: The horizontal line test determines if a function is one-to-one.
For logarithms: d/dx[ln x] = 1/x and d/dx[logₐ x] = 1/(x ln a).
The indefinite integral ∫f(x)dx = F(x) + C, where C is the constant of integration (since derivatives of constants are zero).
Basic antiderivative rules: ∫xⁿdx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C (n ≠ -1), ∫eˣdx = eˣ + C, ∫(1/x)dx = ln|x| + C, ∫cos x dx = sin x + C, ∫sin x dx = -cos x + C.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes One-to-One Functions and Inverses as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about One-to-One Functions and Inverses?

options.text: These three rules, combined with basic derivatives, enable differentiation of virtually any function.
One-to-one functions have inverse functions f⁻¹ where f⁻¹(f(x)) = x.
The chain rule 'peels off layers' of composition: differentiate the outer function (leaving inner unchanged), then multiply by the derivative of the inner.
These diverse applications demonstrate that integration is a universal tool for accumulating continuously varying quantities.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes One-to-One Functions and Inverses as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of One-to-One Functions and Inverses?

options.text: The graph of f⁻¹ is the reflection of f across y = x.
Phase line analysis reveals stability: P = 0 is unstable (arrows point away), P = K is stable (arrows point toward).
Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals introduce integration as the reverse of differentiation.
This yields the derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions: d/dx[arcsin x] = 1/√(1-x²), d/dx[arccos x] = -1/√(1-x²), d/dx[arctan x] = 1/(1+x²).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes One-to-One Functions and Inverses as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on One-to-One Functions and Inverses?

options.text: Solution curves follow the direction of these segments.
These formulas arise from the Pythagorean theorem applied to infinitesimal arc segments: ds = √(dx² + dy²) = √(1 + (dy/dx)²)dx.
The derivative of an inverse function is (f⁻¹)'(a) = 1/f'(f⁻¹(a)), derived from the chain rule applied to f(f⁻¹(x)) = x.
The solution is P(t) = K/(1 + Ae^(-rt)) where A = (K-P₀)/P₀.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes One-to-One Functions and Inverses as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of One-to-One Functions and Inverses?

options.text: Compound interest with variable rates: dA/dt for A = P·e^(∫r(t)dt) requires the chain rule combined with the FTC for variable-rate financial modeling
Engineering: computing cross-sectional area of unusually shaped beams or channels for structural and flow analysis
Radiocarbon dating: ¹⁴C decays with t₁/₂ ≈ 5,730 years; measuring remaining ¹⁴C in organic material determines its age up to ~50,000 years
Cryptography: encryption functions must be one-to-one (so each plaintext maps to a unique ciphertext) and have computable inverses for authorized decryption

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of One-to-One Functions and Inverses.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of One-to-One Functions and Inverses?

options.text: Total rainfall accumulation: if r(t) is the rainfall rate at time t, then total accumulated rainfall by time x is R(x) = ∫₀ˣ r(t)dt
The Squeeze Theorem proving that oscillating functions with decreasing amplitude converge: lim(x→∞) sin(x)/x = 0 since -1/x ≤ sin(x)/x ≤ 1/x
Population dynamics: given a birth rate minus death rate function, calculate total population change over a decade
Temperature conversion: F = (9/5)C + 32 and its inverse C = (5/9)(F - 32) are one-to-one functions used worldwide for unit conversion

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of One-to-One Functions and Inverses.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is One-to-One Functions and Inverses applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: The Squeeze Theorem proving that oscillating functions with decreasing amplitude converge: lim(x→∞) sin(x)/x = 0 since -1/x ≤ sin(x)/x ≤ 1/x
Computing moments of inertia in physics: ∫x²·f(x)dx often requires integration by parts, essential for structural and mechanical engineering
Inverse kinematics in robotics: computing joint angles from desired end-effector position requires inverting the forward kinematic equations
Disease spread: the SIR model's infection curve resembles logistic growth, with the inflection point marking peak new infections

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of One-to-One Functions and Inverses.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of One-to-One Functions and Inverses?

options.text: Radiocarbon dating: ¹⁴C decays with t₁/₂ ≈ 5,730 years; measuring remaining ¹⁴C in organic material determines its age up to ~50,000 years
The Squeeze Theorem proving that oscillating functions with decreasing amplitude converge: lim(x→∞) sin(x)/x = 0 since -1/x ≤ sin(x)/x ≤ 1/x
Modeling the trajectory of a projectile with air resistance: the implicit equation relating position components requires combined chain rule and implicit differentiation
Cryptography: encryption functions must be one-to-one (so each plaintext maps to a unique ciphertext) and have computable inverses for authorized decryption

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of One-to-One Functions and Inverses.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows One-to-One Functions and Inverses in practice?

options.text: Financial mathematics: differentiating revenue functions R(x) to find marginal revenue and determine the optimal quantity to maximize profit
Temperature conversion: F = (9/5)C + 32 and its inverse C = (5/9)(F - 32) are one-to-one functions used worldwide for unit conversion
Newton's method in GPS: iteratively solving nonlinear equations to determine position from satellite distance measurements
Computing moments of inertia in physics: ∫x²·f(x)dx often requires integration by parts, essential for structural and mechanical engineering

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of One-to-One Functions and Inverses.
